ground_truth.title: How to Choose a Tool
ground_truth.author: Karl Voit
ground_truth.main_content: One single tool can not be a perfect fit for all sets of requirements. You need to differ between different situations, frequency of use, preconditions related to the user's knowledge, user interface requirements, and so forth. On the other hand, for each given tool, there exists at least one set of requirements where this particular tool is a perfect fit. Since the requirements are defined by our situation alone, the only free variable here is the tool of choice. Unfortunately, I have to watch people making sub-optimal choices for their software tools too often. Most of the time, the root cause for a bad tool choice is that there was no deliberate process on how to select the tool. In that order. Requirements Analysis of Your Situation: Sit down and do a brainstorming session on the things you want to accomplish. You may then break them down to generalized finer grained requirements if necessary. Choosing a Method: Note that you must not search for a tool or a feature if you are actually looking for a method. Sometimes, there are no methods to search for but for example for email management, todo list management, time management and so forth, you have to look for a method before you're looking for any implementation that matches this method. Locate the most popular tools for a discipline but don't forget to look at small solutions. Please take a careful look on potential lock-in effects. Most probably, you might need to migrate to a different system in future. I've read about too many stories about sad cases where people started to use, e.g., photo managing apps/services like iPhoto or Picasa and ended up with losing many years of photographs or their modifications. This can only be avoided by carefully making your tool choice before actually investing data and time. The outcome of this process so far is a tool which suits your original requirements as best as possible. Only if you do have really good reason, you should modify your requirement analysis results with features you did not think of when coming up with that list in the first place. Your world is changing all the time. So are your requirements. If your tool choice was a wise one, most necessary changes should be solved by configuration. You should re-check for a potential lock-in effect when the tool changes over time. Automatize. If you're doing the same thing all the time, try to find a way of automatize this workflow. Disclaimer: The focus of this method is on tools you are planning to use over a certain period of time. If you need a tool for a short-term project where data export, access and other long-term aspects are not relevant at all: use whatever you feel like.
